question: What randomized controlled trials exist for corneal cross-linking in progressive keratoconus?

41758421.population: Fifty eyes of fifty patients aged 12-35 years with documented progressive keratoconus
41758421.intervention: Accelerated transepithelial (epi-on) corneal collagen cross-linking with Vitamin E TPGS-enhanced riboflavin
41758421.main_findings: Both groups showed significant visual and tomographic improvement at 12 months; epi-on CXL demonstrated stabilization comparable to epi-off CXL with better pachymetric preservation.
41758421.statistical_significance: p < 0.001 for intragroup keratometric flattening; p < 0.05 for intergroup corneal thickness difference

40630024.population: 54 eyes with progressive keratoconus, including thin corneae under 400 micrometres
40630024.intervention: Epithelium-off corneal cross-linking with isoosmolar dextran-based riboflavin
40630024.comparator: Epithelium-off corneal cross-linking with hypoosmolar dextran-free riboflavin
40630024.main_findings: No statistically significant differences between isoosmolar and hypoosmolar groups; the hypoosmolar group required sterile water addition far less often (15% vs 74%).
40630024.statistical_significance: Not statistically significant between riboflavin groups

40340672.population: 140 eyes of 77 patients with paracentral progressive keratoconus
40340672.intervention: Accelerated epithelium-off cross-linking preserving a central 3mm epithelial island
40340672.comparator: Accelerated epithelium-off cross-linking with full epithelium removal over a 9mm central disk
40340672.main_findings: The epithelial-island-preservation group had better early corrected visual acuity, less corneal haze, and less pain; both groups improved significantly at 12 months.
40340672.statistical_significance: Significant difference between groups in early CDVA, haze, and pain (exact p-value not given in the abstract)

stub_query_understanding.search_query: keratoconus AND corneal collagen crosslinking AND randomized controlled trial

41758421.population: Fifty eyes of fifty patients aged 12-35 years with documented progressive keratoconus
41758421.intervention: Accelerated transepithelial (epi-on) corneal collagen cross-linking with Vitamin E TPGS-enhanced riboflavin
41758421.main_findings: Both groups showed significant visual and tomographic improvement at 12 months; epi-on CXL demonstrated stabilization comparable to epi-off CXL with better pachymetric preservation.
41758421.statistical_significance: p < 0.001 for intragroup keratometric flattening; p < 0.05 for intergroup corneal thickness difference

40630024.intervention: Epithelium-off corneal cross-linking with isoosmolar dextran-based riboflavin
40630024.comparator: Epithelium-off corneal cross-linking with hypoosmolar dextran-free riboflavin
40630024.main_findings: No statistically significant differences between isoosmolar and hypoosmolar groups; the hypoosmolar group required sterile water addition far less often (15% vs 74%).
40630024.statistical_significance: Not statistically significant between riboflavin groups

40340672.population: 140 eyes of 77 patients with paracentral progressive keratoconus
40340672.intervention: Accelerated epithelium-off cross-linking preserving a central 3mm epithelial island
40340672.main_findings: The epithelial-island-preservation group had better early corrected visual acuity, less corneal haze, and less pain; both groups improved significantly at 12 months.
40340672.statistical_significance: Significant difference between groups in early CDVA, haze, and pain (exact p-value not given in the abstract)

stub_narrative.evidence_summary: Across three randomized controlled trials of corneal cross-linking for progressive keratoconus, accelerated epi-on and epi-off protocols produced comparable 12-month visual and tomographic stabilization [PMID: 41758421]. Riboflavin osmolarity did not significantly affect outcomes, though hypoosmolar formulations reduced the need for sterile water in thin corneas [PMID: 40630024]. Preserving a central epithelial island during cross-linking improved early comfort and reduced haze without compromising 12-month efficacy in a cohort of 120 patients [PMID: 40340672].
stub_narrative.clinical_implications: Both epi-on and epi-off cross-linking protocols appear viable for halting keratoconus progression; epithelial-sparing techniques may improve early postoperative comfort.
stub_narrative.limitations: All three trials report 12-month outcomes only; longer-term progression and retreatment rates are not addressed.
stub_narrative.future_directions: Longer follow-up and head-to-head trials of epithelial-sparing versus standard epi-off protocols are warranted.
